artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Finally sitting down with a cup of tea while the kids squabble over who gets the blue bowl for yogurt. Figured I’d email instead of texting a novel.

I’ve got three 500g bags of your low‑sugar iron granola (“Maggie blend” is sticking as a name in my head) ready for this weekend, plus two extra for Linda and Carol from your old ward. Same deal as usual: I’ll bring everything when we come down to Red Deer the first weekend of the month, and you can just e‑transfer me the $45 once you’ve divvied it up. Hard to believe I only started tinkering with that iron‑fortified recipe back in March 2023 after your anemia diagnosis at Red Deer Regional.

I’m also packing a couple of 750g “Night Shift Crunch” bags for the night crew from your old unit. The WhatsApp group has been busy this week – I think we’re at six bags at $25 each. I’m still keeping it off the website like they asked, but it makes me happy that it’s helping some of them manage the weight gain and pre‑diabetes stuff from all those years of night shifts.

Life here is the usual juggling act. Evan’s marking social studies projects at the dining table, Liam’s working on a Garneau School poster about Alberta wildlife (he wants to glue granola on as “bear food,” obviously), and Sophie is drawing “granola monsters” again. I booked Maggie (you!) for babysitting duty Saturday night so Evan and I can sneak out for a quick drink on Whyte.

Call me tomorrow so we can sort out timing for pickups – I’m thinking we’ll be at your place by noon.

Love,
Danielle

metadata.subject: Granola run this weekend + kid chaos

artifacts.content:
Working title: Quiet / “off‑menu” product + service lines – pros/cons + what to formalize

1) “Maggie blend” – iron‑fortified, very low sugar
- Developed March 2023 after Mom’s anemia diagnosis at Red Deer Regional Hospital.
- Currently just 500g batches for Mom + her retired nurse friends from her old ward in Red Deer.
- They place informal monthly orders by e‑transfer ($40–$60 total each time) and pick up when I’m in Red Deer the first weekend of each month.
- If I ever make it public, need proper nutrition panel + to be careful about how explicitly I reference anemia.

2) “Night Shift Crunch” – high‑protein, low‑sugar
- Started August 2023 for nurses from Mom’s old unit at Red Deer Regional who were dealing with night‑shift weight gain + pre‑diabetes.
- Sold only through private WhatsApp group at $25 per 750g bag (they literally track who owes me what in the chat).
- If formalized, strip out all references to pre‑diabetes/weight gain and frame as generic “sustaining high‑protein blend for busy schedules.”

3) Garneau / ADHD parfait kits
- Since Oct 2023: weekly “school‑safe” granola + yogurt parfait kits for three families from Liam’s Grade 2 class at Garneau School.
- Portion into individually labeled containers with kids’ names + texture notes from the pediatric dietitian at Stollery Children’s Hospital (ADHD + sensory issues).
- Need to think through food‑safety + liability if this ever scales beyond this tiny, word‑of‑mouth group.

Overall: all three are meaningful but emotionally heavy offerings; make sure I’m not overextending.

Event: Sunday prep – Garneau kits + market planning

Time: Every Sunday, 4:00–6:00 p.m.
Location: Home kitchen (83 Ave apartment)
Attendees: Danielle (primary), occasional “helpers” Liam & Sophie

Notes:

1) Garneau ADHD/sensory parfait kits (since Oct 2023)
- Prep weekly “school‑safe” granola + yogurt parfait kits for three families from Liam’s Grade 2 class at Garneau School.
- Follow individual instructions from the pediatric dietitian at the Stollery Children’s Hospital (texture, portion size, timing during the school day).
- Label each container clearly with child’s name + any notes (e.g., “no visible seeds,” “extra smooth yogurt”).
- Double‑check that granola used is nut‑free School‑Safe Crunch.

2) Allergy‑group stock‑up (seasonal)
- Before back‑to‑school: set aside 6–8 bags of School‑Safe Crunch for ~12 families from the Stollery peanut‑free allergy support group (joined April 2024 after Liam’s reaction to that store‑bought bar at Garneau).
- Remember Jenna’s daughter’s batch needs to be from clearly segregated ingredients/line, as she has multiple anaphylactic allergies.

3) Farmers’ market + wholesale planning
- Review Old Strathcona Farmers’ Market inventory, especially Maple Pecan Prairie Blend and Honey Berry Sunrise.
- Check standing orders from independent grocers + any new inquiries from Old Strathcona Business Association contacts.
- Sketch Instagram content for the week (Reels ideas, kid “taste‑test” clips, etc.).

Remind Evan to keep kids out of the kitchen during labeling so I can focus.

Subject: Catch-up + Calgary caregiver workshop brain swirl

Hey Em,

I’m hiding in the bedroom while Liam and Sophie build a “granola fort” out of Prairie Harvest boxes, so perfect time to write.

First: thank you again for roping in your Foothills crew back in fall 2022 when Evan’s hours were cut at McKernan and we were two months behind on the mortgage. I still can’t believe you started that group text with five of your nurse colleagues and convinced them to each order at least $50 of granola every month. Those ICU nurse orders have quietly become one of my steadiest online customer groups.

On the Calgary side: are we still good for the next caregiver granola workshop near Alberta Children’s? Since February 2024, these quarterly 8‑person sessions have become one of my favourite things, but I always get nervous beforehand. For this one, can you confirm with the outpatient eating disorder team that parents are allowed to bring copies of their kids’ meal plans? I want to be able to help them tweak my recipes to hit the calorie and fat targets without stepping over any privacy or clinical boundaries.

I’m thinking of doing three base blends (one higher‑fat, one higher‑protein, one super energy‑dense) and letting them customize with mix‑ins that fit the Alberta Children’s Hospital guidelines. I’ll bring measuring cups and a simple worksheet so they can re‑create the blends at home.

Also, how are you feeling with July creeping closer? I keep picturing tiny you in scrubs juggling a newborn and night shifts. I’ll block off a weekend to come down after the baby arrives, workshop or no workshop.

Love you,
Dani

metadata.subject: Catch-up + Calgary caregiver workshop brain swirl

artifacts.content:
Subject: McKernan burnout blend + other school things

Hey Ev,

While you’re at your staff meeting, I’m finalizing the label proof for the McKernan “Burnout Recovery Blend” and wanted to run a couple things by you.

Can you double‑check with Sarah Jenkins that it’s still okay to include the staff mental health resource URL on the back of all 200 single‑serve snack packs? When we first talked in June 2024, she mentioned the spike in stress leaves and how they were quietly trying to reduce sick days and turnover, but I don’t want to step on HR toes by being too on‑the‑nose about burnout.

On a related note, Karen Mitchell emailed me the sweetest note about how my low‑sugar Maple Pecan helped her manage her gestational diabetes last fall. Apparently she mentioned it during the January 2025 staff wellness meeting, and now I’ve got a wave of pregnant teachers and support staff ordering 4–5 bags at a time. Your school is turning into its own customer segment.

Logistics: if I drop the “Burnout Recovery” boxes at McKernan’s front office next Thursday around 2:30, can you help haul them to the staff room? I’ll be coming straight from the shared kitchen in Ritchie after a big Maple Pecan / Honey Berry run.

Kid stuff: Liam’s Garneau project on “communities” now includes a whole section on “McKernan School teachers who eat Mom’s granola,” which made me laugh and also feel weirdly proud. Sophie wants to come to your classroom again to draw “teacher granola monsters.”

Text me after your meeting and let me know what Sarah says.

Love,
D

metadata.subject: McKernan burnout blend + other school things

artifacts.content:
Subject: How anxiety group + Prairie Harvest keep colliding

Hi Dr. Singh,

I wanted to jot this down while it’s fresh in my mind, before tonight’s bedtime chaos wipes my brain.

After last week’s outpatient anxiety group at the University of Alberta Hospital, Melissa (the HR coordinator from that downtown engineering firm) caught me in the hallway. I’d shared a bit in group about how hard it’s been to keep Prairie Harvest Granola Co. afloat while dealing with panic attacks and the constant fear of overextending the business. On the way out, she asked more about what I make and then emailed the next day to order 200 branded snack packs for their staff mental health awareness week in June 2023.

On one hand, I’m grateful – it’s exactly the kind of corporate client I need, and the timing is great with the kids’ summer camps and our emergency fund still recovering from that surprise car repair last year. On the other hand, it feels strange having my anxiety support network overlap with my customer base. Part of me worries that I’m “performing” my panic in group because I know it might also lead to networking, which feels gross.

This all sits on top of the older stress from when Evan’s hours were cut at McKernan in Sept 2022 and we fell two months behind on the mortgage. My sister Emily rallying her Foothills ICU colleagues to each order $50/month of granola was a lifeline, but it also reinforced this idea that my worth is tied to how much granola I can sell.

I’d like to talk about boundaries around sharing business stuff in group, and how to separate financial relief from emotional support.

Thanks,
Danielle

metadata.subject: How anxiety group + Prairie Harvest keep colliding

artifacts.content:
Subject: School parents, allergies, and this whole granola village

Hi Mom,

Quick update while I wait for a batch of Honey Berry Sunrise to finish in the Ritchie ovens.

You’d get a kick out of how much my customer base has turned into “people connected to the kids’ schools.” After I brought sample bags of nut‑free School‑Safe Crunch to Liam’s Grade 2 Halloween party at Garneau on October 31, 2024, his teacher Ms. Reynolds and three of the moms (including Priya, whose son Arjun has that severe peanut allergy) started ordering regularly. At this point, I’m pretty sure around 40% of my monthly direct sales are just Garneau and Sophie’s Old Strathcona co‑op preschool parents.

Then there’s the Stollery side: after Liam’s scary reaction to that store‑bought granola bar at Garneau, we joined the peanut‑free allergy support group at the Stollery Children’s Hospital in April 2024. One of the moms, Jenna, posted in our private Facebook group in May that School‑Safe Crunch was the only granola she trusted enough to send in her daughter’s lunch (multiple anaphylactic allergies). Since then, about a dozen families from that group have become regulars, often buying 6–8 bags at a time before the school year starts.

It’s a lot of pressure, honestly – knowing these parents are trusting me with their kids’ safety – but it also feels like exactly why I started Prairie Harvest in the first place: simple ingredients, clear labels, and being a real person they can message if they’re worried.

Anyway, timer’s beeping. Call you tonight after bedtime stories.

Love,
Dani

metadata.subject: School parents, allergies, and this whole granola village
